speaker: interviewer
text: --Mathis in Los Angeles, California in the United States of America on February the 20th, 1996. So Mr. Mathis, you were just telling us that the Judenrat put your mother on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And luckily, you were able to get her off. She was able to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My uncle did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, OK, she stayed-- she was in city. [INAUDIBLE] not be there because they go slowly, slowly. They started-- they have to go in. They make all-- all the time less people, less people. Now it's this much. And next week, another more. More and more until they get finished. After all, long time, she was-- they left her. You can do nothing. You cannot kick her out because my uncle works for them, and I work for my uncle. He said, if we don't work, then take [INAUDIBLE]. I said, I'm going with my mother. Then they open again. Open again. Then open-- they make it Judenrein. Everybody got to go. Nobody's going to leave. Then in the night, they knocked on the door. Everybody's to go now-- on the [INAUDIBLE] next to the temple, so the shul. Over there was a place. Everybody have to come together. And over there I went. My uncle was over there. I've talked to my uncle. I'm going [INAUDIBLE] to me. I'll take my mother too. They took away my mother. My mother went to get my sister. My mother said, take-- take-- [CRYING] take away. I said, they can't do nothing. They no listen to me. Then they take to my mother, [INAUDIBLE] take much, take [? Dvora ?] too. Then the Gestapo take a gun, a rifle, and knocked her over the head like this. Now I cannot help them. For this time, I cannot stay no more in the house. Then-- OK, then they took-- they took her away. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sister took away with mother. They got-- well, then they took her back to work. [INAUDIBLE] leftover. A little but not much people left over to work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Then I went to my-- my girlfriend, my girlfriend. And I stayed with-- with her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is [INAUDIBLE]. Then they used to took for me [INAUDIBLE], to work-- to work. The-- [INAUDIBLE] feed us, everything. I used to stay with them. Nothing to do. After all, [? this is not ?] nothing. The Germans, they go like this, slowly, slowly, until they liquidated the whole thing. Liquidation from everywhere. Then last time, they said, the city got to be a Judenrein. No Jews no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what I to do? I hiding with my [INAUDIBLE], my brother and [INAUDIBLE] in their basement. Midnight, used to go around the Jewish [INAUDIBLE]. They holler. Every-- every-- go out. Don't stay there. When they find over there, if you're hiding, [INAUDIBLE] nothing. The Jews, everybody's going to be Judenrein. No more Jews in this city. And if they see you, they're gonna kill you anyway. Why you do it? [? Get out ?] better.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, to my friend, [INAUDIBLE]. Going out. Going out. [INAUDIBLE] got to transport. [? Going ?] [? from ?] transport, going [INAUDIBLE] transport, [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yiddish, we said [YIDDISH], Polish, [POLISH]. Decided, OK. What can you do? My uncle-- my uncle was-- and he worked for them. They paid him back. They didn't pay him back to take him to gas chamber. No. They don't take him to gas chamber. Before, I'm going to tell you later about this. They took us on a way of there from-- from our city to [? Miedzyrzecz ?] was about 25 kilometer. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? Germans. ?] The Germans let us [? go. ?] The Germans watch us. The Germans that was going with us, the Gestapo. We don't go by ourselves. Oh, no, no, no. Let's go to-- they said-- they said, before you go to the gas chamber, they take you there. Oh no. They take you [? over ?] [? to ?] the ghetto. Over there was [INAUDIBLE] with [INAUDIBLE] around. You cannot go out. Now, way back over there, the Jews-- a lot the Jews are lost for now. You know, people-- you know-- not-- [INAUDIBLE], the people used to work for-- I don't know how to say it here-- like truck driver-- truck driver, other things. They want to make a attack of the Germans. The-- the women start to cry. Don't do it. They will kill us. Because they want to do it. They run away to fields in [? partisan. ?] They said, no. Don't do it. And then [INAUDIBLE], put us in the ghetto. You cannot go out. They kill for time. You have to [? see ?] all the time. Then we started to make a-- [INAUDIBLE] how you call a hiding place, [YIDDISH] in Yiddish. Hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean like a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Comes back a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Hiding place. How you do it-- from the wall, you make a extra wall and then pull out long-- long board, and put-- you go in and covered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In [? Reach? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, with strangers. With my cousin-- oh, with my cousin, with my-- and them. With my girlfriend's parents. They-- the-- the father died before the war. With her mother and brothers and [INAUDIBLE], all of them. They were hiding together with us and-- and other people. Then somebody told them about us. I don't know. [INAUDIBLE]. They took him out, and they know [? where ?] and we're still alive. [INAUDIBLE] took us out-- out. The Gestapo with their guns like this. They said, [GERMAN]. Run, to run. [INAUDIBLE] center of the city, sitting on their knees, everybody. Everybody sitting on their knees from the morning, almost all day, sitting on the [INAUDIBLE] over there. I know the street, even. Then he goes around, around, and look what to do with them. Which one to take out right away and which one to take some later. Then they took out my uncle, [? Soha. ?] He worked for them. They paid him back. Put up the wall. He shot him. [CRYING] They said to him, we not going to send you to Treblinka. We can-- we finish you here. And they kill him right away. Then I sitting next to my-- [INAUDIBLE] a girl from [INAUDIBLE]. She was a pretty girl. I remember her name, [? Shita. ?] They took here. They shoot her right away next to me. Never so lucky. I don't know. I was lucky they didn't shoot me. My heart [INAUDIBLE]. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Left the wall. They come over. They decide to take us all over in-- in the cemetery because they'd rather dig the ditches to bury-- to bury us. I went to the cemetery in [INAUDIBLE], going with him. I know they can see. What can you do? You go behind us, Gestapo with guns and [INAUDIBLE] with their rifles. You can't do nothing. After all, the [INAUDIBLE] order to take us back out. I said, out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took us on the train. Train-- cattle train-- not a real train, cattle train. Close up, locked up, and then they took us over there. Takes about two days to take us around with no water or nothing. People died on the train. You never believe it. My uncle-- cousin [INAUDIBLE]. People sit on him. And I was myself running next to a little-- little opening to get air-- and can still breathe. Then they took us to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know how many people. Cannot move around. How many people? I don't know how many people. Cannot move around. Who I'm going to count? I count the people? I don't know how to count even. My-- my brain doesn't work for this no more. My mind was different. How long I'm gonna live? Maybe I'm gonna die. They took us over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, at this time, I was about around-- about 20 years old, 21. Because the [INAUDIBLE] was, they [? didn't ?] took me to war. The Polish because-- Poland-- eight days was-- was-- was lost the war. Eight days was gone Poland. If they had more time, they would've taken me too. They got no time for this to take me to war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish-- you know, Polish people was-- how you call it? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sell the Poland to the [INAUDIBLE]. The-- the Germans airplanes was flying. They shot-- they shot [? them ?] [INAUDIBLE] where to [? bomber. ?] A lot of them no good. I mean, Poland was no good. [INAUDIBLE] was spies, was spies. Polish people were spies against them own self. Do you remember what year this was when they took you to Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majdanek, they took me in 1942. 1942. I think in 1942 because I got written-- I check myself. I'd remember. I don't have to [INAUDIBLE], maybe before this. I remembered so many things. So many-- I don't know. I don't know. I think I lost my mind. Comes back. Still got nightmares. I cannot sleep in the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes I think they run after me in the night. Well, I said, [INAUDIBLE] my wife, what's going to you? Yes. So many years ago. Got the past very bad. Then in Majdanek, come to Majdanek, was a sign. They opened the doors. And I said, can I get a drink? They said, yeah. Don't worry. You get a drink. It's gonna be right away. We call them [INAUDIBLE]. You can go into gas chamber. But [INAUDIBLE] was a sign over there. [SPEAKING GERMAN], in Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: The work in Majdanek was not work over there. Was hard labor. They take me with another man, take a board on my-- my shoulder, the other-- the other side from the other shoulder and put on rocks and this. They run after this with-- with a-- with a stick, a rubber stick like this, knocked [INAUDIBLE]. Oh. Then they took us-- one time took us out from there, took us out from the Majdanek to work. I forgot the name of it. Over there was Jewish living. I was over there to clean up this place. That's-- oh boy. Everything was [? hot even. ?] Children's-- children's bed, everything, and food-- [INAUDIBLE]. They took them away to Majdanek, to burn. [INAUDIBLE], Majdanek was a-- the-- the-- the women's place, another barrack, another place. I used to go to the wires and talk. In Majdanek, they give us work. You're never going to believe that kind of work. We used to clean off the toilets, put it over [? one, ?] and clean the [INAUDIBLE] on the fields. And I used to go and do it like this to make it soft, the ground, from the toilets with these men. Men did the work thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me just take you back a little bit. When you first arrived at Majdanek, what did they do with you when you first got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us-- I-- I figured they going to take us right away-- oh, I forget-- they took us clean up, put in-- put in a pool something. Burnt so much the skin. I figured they were-- before this, I figure they gonna take us to gas chamber. They took a [INAUDIBLE], my cousin away, everybody. Children-- I had my two cousins, little 10 years, 12 years, never seen [? work. ?] Took here. There. Here. There. One on this side. One on this side. We never know who got to die, who got to live. I didn't know where I'm going. They never came back. I never see them. Even Majdanek, every night they took down people. They don't give them to eat nothing. They took all the time appel [INAUDIBLE] on the fields. They count the people if they are still there. They look. It's a funny count. They know that we never can run. They know maybe somebody died. They put on somebody-- if-- I cannot explain everything. Better let it go. They terrible.

words.text: They don't give them to eat

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you first arrived, they gave you like a disinfectant bath.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else happened when you first got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Take you right away and give you a place to stay, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, one over the top from the others. Nothing, not good. Like-- like-- like for horses. Not barrack. This a barrack like for horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? They give you just in the morning, and that's it. If you don't eat this, you got nothing. Sometime I go in the night, I come down. You have to go down sometime. And I said to my cousin-- he was my cousin, the picture you got, [INAUDIBLE] for them. He's [? laying on me. ?] I said, [INAUDIBLE]. Watch my piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come up, no more bread. Because why? He smoked. Me need it. I didn't smoke. I never smoke, and I needed bread. And I got nothing. Now you have to suffer. Even my cousin, you know, [? never care. ?] [INAUDIBLE] don't care [? if he's a ?] cousin [INAUDIBLE]. He took it. I said, [INAUDIBLE], why did you? I didn't. I know he did it. What can I do? Later the next day, take out a piece of bread. And little piece of bread all day. That's it. No more. Who was watching over you in these barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over you, they had the kapos. How you call the kapos? A leader from them, kapos. The kapos. They watching you. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they like? What they like? They like the Gestapo. Even is a Jewish kapo. I never trust them anyway. Even I used to go out-- the Jewish [INAUDIBLE] leader, some they give out little water, like a soup. Was not soup. I don't what this even was. Maybe it was in the bottom [INAUDIBLE] a little better, even from the top. I said to him, why you [INAUDIBLE]? You know, people was in the war, even you can be the closest to you, was terrible. Was not good. Was not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not good. Nothing ever [INAUDIBLE]. They don't like [INAUDIBLE] people better. We'll come to this later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But while you were in Majdanek, were you ever treated badly? Were you beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, badly. Oh yeah. Yeah, I was not very long because later on, they were looking for younger people to work, out-- out to work-- to take them to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you-- how long were you actually in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you're there, did you ever see people taken to the gas chamber? Were you aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes! Oh, yeah. Put them on the-- put them on a wagon. Put them on a wagon, right away [? from ?] the wagon. They kill them already on the-- on fields-- how you call it? They call it like, like the soldiers stays and count them. How you call [INAUDIBLE]? Ah. [INAUDIBLE] something. [INAUDIBLE] they counted and take them [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] come, they take the [? tents ?] out to kill. They did daily all the time, every time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see people being killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [? Nothing ?] [INAUDIBLE] killed. Put them on the wagon right away to burn them. Yeah, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about at night while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. What I think? I'm next. What I think? What I think? You think I'm going-- it's not just [INAUDIBLE]. You couldn't fathom what I think. There was more concentration [INAUDIBLE]. Then from this, they-- they started to take out-- take out to work. My cousin was still alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us out, [INAUDIBLE] to the Germans, Gestapo, looking who was good, take off everything, the clothes. Then they go around to see how you look. My cousin was a little-- actually and before the war-- one foot was a little shorter. [INAUDIBLE] limped onto one foot, this side. He started to kiss me. That's it. I'm not to see him. On this side, this side. Then they figure I'm a good slave for work. That's not happened here before. They took-- took a-- put everybody in a line and see. [INAUDIBLE] before this happened, they count. The [? tent ?] [INAUDIBLE], and I look from the side. Something is wrong. I'm afraid I'm in the [? tent, ?] and they're going to get me out for killing for nothing. Then I started myself. I tell you. Everybody heard the same thing. I started to take my shoes, my shoes-- tie my shoes, [? whether ?] they are together. Then I go on the other side. I rescue myself. If I be the [? tent, ?] they come to [? the tent-- ?] the [? tent-- ?] it out, I'll be dead. I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can I do? Everybody fight themself. Who know? My cousin, he took my bread. He don't care about me. I am hungry all day. All day, more than a day. Oh, I don't-- I don't know it. I'm in the [? tent. ?] I was afraid, and I did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when-- then when they took to checking who's got good for work, I went out, they took me to work. OK. From this time on, they send-- they send out from Majdanek. They gave us a bread on the way to go there. I don't know where they send us. They send us to-- to Skarzysko. There-- over there was a factory, ammunition factory. I don't know. I go with-- with the truth. Sometime the truth's not good to tell. I tell I'm a tailor. I think I can work here. Maybe they need it for a tailor. They give me worst. Was two camps over there-- A, B, C. The worst camps was C. They give me to C. Still in trouble. Still in trouble. My wife was in different. She was in A.

speaker: interviewer
text: My wife over there. What can I do? First of all, [INAUDIBLE] first time, I went over there. They do the same thing. They make [INAUDIBLE], this and this. Give you [? to dress. ?] Over there, it was more-- [? it's in ?] the camp. Over there the leaders was Jews. Now, when you left Majdanek--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you went to Skarzysko--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you stay at Skarzysko? Or did you go back and forth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Skarzysko was a longer time. Longer time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually slept there, and you went to live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- before we talk about that, can you please just describe for us what the camp of Majdanek looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it look like?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what it was like-- how many barracks? Did it have wires?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks? You never-- you think I'm gonna count the barracks? I never count in my life. I cannot count-- even- I cannot count barracks. I was not sure of my life tomorrow. What-- what interested me the barracks if I can't do nothing? If I [INAUDIBLE], I [? figure, ?] you know, [INAUDIBLE] the people. I can't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it surrounded by electric wires?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh. You can do nothing. You cannot go out of this. Never. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of guards were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gestapo. Around [INAUDIBLE] with rifles, all around. Like-- was like here in a jail-- worse. All around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took you out from there for-- for labor. For what kind labor? The work in the-- they don't need the labor. Just to beat you up, all the time. Sometimes we [? can ?] appel to go outside, and they said, run! Run!

speaker: interviewer
text: I did sometime [INAUDIBLE]. Not in this line, not in Majdanek. In other-- other camps had it different. What can I do? When you left Majdanek, how did you get to Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: They pick. I don't know how I get in train. Oh, they-- they-- going from Majdanek to Skarzysko, they give you bread. Yeah. They treat you right on the way. [INAUDIBLE] where you going because they need you for work over there. Oh, this was work? Hell-- hell, better than this work, what I got. First of all-- first time they took me to Skarzysko, the work not so bad. I used to work in a factory for ammunition. They took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE] ammunition, put on-- I don't know you call this-- [INAUDIBLE] ammunition [INAUDIBLE] to make like a-- oh boy. Take-- I don't know how to explain this, how you call this. All kind of work, and I did it right. I can do it-- [INAUDIBLE] I did right. The only thing, later on, the people-- a lot the people work with transport-- [INAUDIBLE] trains, put the ammunition on the trains to the war. They die fast. Some people work [INAUDIBLE], some other work in factory was some [INAUDIBLE] like green stuff. This is poison. They die fast. [INAUDIBLE] they die fast, they take-- they take people from the easy work back to this. That's-- then one night, I see-- working over there in the factory, I see camps of Gestapo in the night. You know, it's [INAUDIBLE] like this. The first night, they took me to [? Colditz. ?] Yeah. This is what happened-- because after my mind comes back to tell you the truth, because I don't know what's happen. It goes back.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the night, they come over there, looking for other people out for the hard labor on the trains. The people that were working with me was from Lodz. They know me-- all girls know me. They said, [INAUDIBLE]! [INAUDIBLE]! Run away. [INAUDIBLE] [? hiding ?] [? you. ?] They can't find us. But what can I do? Where can I hide them? We cannot move no place.

speaker: interviewee
text: They pick me up. They pick me because I was young, looking good. They pick me to the work. And this was hard labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same night, they took me to the-- to working, and I wait from this place. I work [INAUDIBLE]. This work was-- the name from this factory was [? Schmitz ?] Company, [? Schmitz ?] Factory, was OK. Over there, at-- at the front-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a policeman, a Jewish policeman, [INAUDIBLE]. He was from [INAUDIBLE]. He was very good to me. He liked me. He asked me to [INAUDIBLE] because I did work for him. He was very good. And at night, he give me something more, more food to eat and [INAUDIBLE]. Then I did-- when they kick me out, they seem right. They transport-- I'm not just a transport. My hand was for tailor, not-- cannot carry-- was bullets like this for the-- for the tanks. I cannot do it. The Polish people-- Polish-- he knocked me almost to death in night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after all, I started to talk to him. You know, I'm a kravits. Kravits in Polish-- you know a kravits is a tailor-- a tailor. I can do this. Do this. I can do it for you. I started to soft him down. He started to be different. I have to do something. Otherwise, I'm dead. What can I do? Yeah. I did this. And this was the last [INAUDIBLE] over there. Have to go every night. All night working [INAUDIBLE]. Just in paper, I did my-- my leg like this. I cry. I said to [? Mother, ?] I don't want to live no more. I want to die. Work in the night of transport and cold. In the night, it frost in Poland. It's not like here. I can't stand it no more. I cannot stand it. [INAUDIBLE] nothing. Where can I go to die or-- or to do it? Forget it. Drop dead, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time-- I don't know when this is happening-- then they took me after this-- I have to push a [INAUDIBLE], transport, log on the transport from the-- from the train. I have to push the wagons-- another put people. And then behind you, a Gestapo, and he knocks you to death. Faster! Faster! [INAUDIBLE] [? Schnell, ?] they said to [? Schnell ?] in Deutsch, faster, faster. We can't do faster. I was too mad to run away. Run away, he kill me. And then he run after me-- he run after me. I run to the factory, the Jewish working there, through the factory, run out the factory. And they ask the factory, you see this-- they didn't know me everybody. He said, we don't know if seen nobody. Cannot catch me. I run a-- a toilet. I sleep all night in toilet, and I was afraid tomorrow to go to-- to go back to the-- the camp. I was afraid that he did it. This [? work ?] not good. Was all [INAUDIBLE] good. I was-- I rescued myself. That-- that's it. I cannot stand it no more. Kill, that's it. They never going to [? kill him. ?] Everybody knows me over in the lager. Everybody. Because I did good, very [? bad. ?] [INAUDIBLE] I do good. The same thing then what's happened. Oh boy. Oh boy! I have to remember everything. This is in the [INAUDIBLE]. I got a history. I can never [INAUDIBLE]. My children don't like for me to tell them this. OK. This-- there I make it from that. I walk-- I walk over there until I-- until they take me away from there. One time-- and over there, in the same camp was a lot of people not far from our city-- [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], name it. They take the little towns. They know me, everybody. I used to go over there to barber. Some people, they don't like it. People who come from Majdanek to this later, they don't like it. The Jewish don't like it. They call the Majdanek, they call them with names. They don't call me because I don't know. They like me. I don't know. I tell you the truth. They like me. I did it. I did for the police Jewish [? woman ?] sometime. And sometime, I was sitting-- they took me in a Jewish-- in-- in jail, in camp. And he comes-- the Gestapo comes, and you're sitting here because you don't want to go to work. They look on, and I was hiding under the [? bridge ?] in the bathroom. And I know this. And [? go ?] me with a cane to see if they can catch me. I see the cane going there, I go and run on the other side. They can do nothing. Then they find me out, and they put [? me ?] over there in the jail. They call this a jail. I don't know what this is. And the Gestapo comes later, and takes off in the-- that-- they take you of a place and shoot you and good-bye. And then, I know from-- from the-- the-- land leader, was-- he tooks care of the people, that are sick. And I know him. I know [? him's wife. ?] I know him. I do work. I said-- I holler-- I holler through the window and said, please, go tell this man I'm-- I'm in jail. They did it. They come and take [INAUDIBLE]. If they didn't took me out this day, I would be dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was is man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He don't live. He was a leader from-- from Skarzysko too. He took me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I cannot remember. I know it. I don't think he lives. He was an older man. He-- he was-- oh, he was good, always good to me. I'll tell you. Now, [INAUDIBLE] people-- people are going to a [INAUDIBLE] in Landsberg, in Skarzysko. They [INAUDIBLE] because they did-- did something. One time they took me-- they took us every Sunday from [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE]. They take a bet over there because they [INAUDIBLE] it was nothing, except they take [? the worker. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was a tailor, but I need thread. I need something to work. I got a little needle. And also I got a little scissors, nail scissors. That's what I use, all. What I do with thread? They have cases of it, that they used to be packed, the ammunition. The handles looks like thread. They cut it off, and this is sabotage. You know what I mean sabotage? I know in English it's sabotage. I bought this. I don't cut it. I bought it for something else. Then they knew, and cut it. Then before-- before, we have to go back to [INAUDIBLE]. They-- they check everybody, and my heart was like this, pumped like this. I was afraid because I was-- it hiding places, and they cannot find me. And I was lucky. I passed the test. [INAUDIBLE].
